content:
# Genesis Chapter 15 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 8v–9r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_015_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_015_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 10–11 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** After these things the word of the LORD came to Abram in a vision, saying, "Fear not, Abram, I am your shield; your reward shall be very great."

**2** But Abram said, "O Lord GOD, what will you give me, for I continue childless, and the heir of my house is Eliezer of Damascus?"

**3** And Abram said, "Behold, you have given me no offspring, and a member of my household will be my heir."

**4** And behold, the word of the LORD came to him, saying, "This man shall not be your heir; your very own son shall be your heir."

**5** And he brought him outside and said, "Look toward heaven, and number the stars, if you are able to number them." Then he said to him, "So shall your offspring be."

**6** And he believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness.

**7** And he said to him, "I am the LORD who brought you out from Ur of the Chaldeans to give you this land to possess it."

**8** But he said, "O Lord GOD, how am I to know that I shall possess it?"

**9** He said to him, "Bring me a heifer three years old, a female goat three years old, a ram three years old, a turtledove, and a young pigeon."

**10** And he brought him all these, and he cut them in two and laid each half over against the other. But he did not cut the birds in two.

**11** And when birds of prey came down upon the carcasses, Abram drove them away.

**12** When the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell upon Abram. And behold, dreadful and great darkness fell upon him.

**13** Then the LORD said to Abram, "Know for certain that your offspring will be sojourners in a land that is not theirs and will serve the people there, and they will be afflicted for four hundred years.

**14** But I will also bring judgment on the nation that they serve, and afterward they shall come out with great possessions.

**15** As for you, you shall go to your fathers in peace; you shall be buried in a good old age.

**16** And they shall come back here in the fourth generation, for the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete."

**17** When the sun had gone down and it was dark, behold, a smoking fire pot and a flaming torch passed between these pieces.

**18** On that day the LORD made a covenant with Abram, saying, "To your offspring I give this land, from the river of Egypt to the great river, the river Euphrates,

**19** the land of the Kenites, the Kenizzites, the Kadmonites,

**20** the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Rephaim,

**21** the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Girgashites and the Jebusites."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 15 spans Folios 8v–9r.

   **Folio 8v** (GEN_015_page_001.jpg): This folio carries the end of Genesis 14 in the right-hand columns (including the conclusion of the Melchizedek episode and the dialogue with the king of Sodom through verse 24), with Genesis 15 opening in the leftward columns. The characteristic three-column Masoretic layout of the Leningrad Codex is clearly visible. The opening of Genesis 15 — אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה ("after these things") — marks the beginning of a new pericope following the setumah section marker that closes chapter 14. The opening formula הָיָה דְבַר יְהוָה אֶל אַבְרָם ("the word of the LORD came to Abram") is legible in the characteristic Masoretic script, as is the first divine speech: אַל תִּירָא אַבְרָם אָנֹכִי מָגֵן לָךְ ("Fear not, Abram, I am your shield"). Abram's lament concerning his childlessness (vv. 2–3), the promise of an heir from his own body (v. 4), the stellar promise (v. 5), and the critical verse 6 — וְהֶאֱמִן בַּיהוָה וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לּוֹ צְדָקָה ("and he believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness") — are legible in the central column. The land covenant dialogue (vv. 7–8) and the divine instruction to bring the animals (v. 9) continue through the folio, running into the covenant ceremony (vv. 10–11) and the account of the deep sleep falling upon Abram (v. 12) near the lower portion of the folio.

   **Folio 9r** (GEN_015_page_002.jpg): This folio opens with the divine speech prophesying the Egyptian sojourn (vv. 13–16), with the numbers אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה ("four hundred years," v. 13) and the reference to the fourth generation (הַדּוֹר הָרְבִיעִי, v. 16) legible. The climactic theophany of the smoking fire pot (תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן) and the flaming torch (לַפִּיד אֵשׁ) passing between the pieces (v. 17) is visible in the upper columns. The formal covenant declaration of verse 18 — בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא כָּרַת יְהוָה אֶת אַבְרָם בְּרִית ("on that day the LORD made a covenant with Abram") — along with the river boundaries from Egypt to the Euphrates (נַחַל מִצְרַיִם...הַנָּהָר הַגָּדֹל נְהַר פְּרָת, v. 18) is legible. The concluding list of ten peoples whose land is included in the covenant grant (vv. 19–21) closes the chapter, and the folio continues into the opening of Genesis 16. The Masoretic section markers at the close of Genesis 15 are visible.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Page 10 (GEN_015_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the tail end of Genesis 14 and the opening portion of Genesis 15 in its three-column uncial format, with the section marker "Γ CAP. XV" visible at the right margin, indicating the chapter division. The LXX Genesis 15 text in Vaticanus covers the divine speech and vision (vv. 1–6), the land promise and the covenant preparation (vv. 7–10), through approximately verse 12. Page 11 (GEN_015_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) continues with the theophany and land-grant promises, concluding the chapter. The chapter marker "CAP. XVIII" is visible at the top of this second folio — confirming the progression through the manuscript. The LXX text agrees with the MT in the broad outline of the chapter, with the principal variants noted in the table below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. דְּבַר יְהוָה — "The Word of the LORD" (v. 1) — First Use of the Prophetic Formula**

The opening formula of Genesis 15 — הָיָה דְבַר יְהוָה אֶל אַבְרָם בַּמַּחֲזֶה (hayah devar YHWH el Avram bamachazeh, "the word of the LORD came to Abram in a vision") — is the first occurrence of the phrase דְּבַר יְהוָה (devar YHWH, "the word of the LORD") in the Hebrew Bible. This formula will become the standard opening of prophetic oracles throughout the Hebrew Bible: it occurs more than 240 times in the prophetic corpus and serves as the technical introduction to divine revelation received by a prophet. Its appearance here constitutes an implicit identification of Abram as a prophet — one who receives the devar YHWH in the same mode as Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and the Twelve. This is confirmed by Genesis 20:7, where God explicitly calls Abram a prophet (נָבִיא, navi).

The word בַּמַּחֲזֶה (bamachazeh, "in a vision") derives from the root חָזָה (chazah, "to see, to behold, to envision"). A מַחֲזֶה (machazeh) is a visionary seeing — not a dream (חֲלוֹם, chalom) but a waking vision, or at least a state distinct from ordinary perception. The same word is used of Balaam's oracles in Numbers 24:4, 16. The formal translation renders "in a vision" — the standard English equivalent, preserving the technical character of the term.

The LXX renders: ἐγενήθη ῥῆμα κυρίου πρὸς Αβραμ ἐν ὁράματι (egenethē rhema kyriou pros Abram en horamati, "the word of the Lord came to Abram in a vision"). The LXX's ὅραμα (horama, "vision, sight") is a transparent equivalent of machazeh. The formal translation follows the MT and LXX in agreement: "in a vision."

**2. מָגֵן / שָׂכָר — "Shield" and "Reward" (v. 1) — The Double Crux**

The first divine speech contains the most discussed grammatical crux in Genesis 15:

אָנֹכִי מָגֵן לָךְ שְׂכָרְךָ הַרְבֵּה מְאֹד
(anoki magen lak sekharkha harbeh me'od)

This clause has been read in at least two ways:

**(a) The Predicate Reading (traditional):** "I am your shield; your reward shall be very great." On this reading, God makes two parallel promises: (1) I myself am your shield (מָגֵן, magen) — a protector; and (2) your reward (שָׂכָר, sakhar) — the compensation or recompense for what you have done — shall be very great. This reading takes the two nouns (shield / reward) as two separate predications. The formal translation adopts this reading, following the Masoretic accents, which mark a division between the two clauses.

**(b) The Appositional Reading:** "I am your shield, your very great reward." On this reading, the phrase "your very great reward" stands in apposition to "your shield" — both describe God himself. God is not only shield but also the reward: the promise is not what God will give but that God himself is the gift. This reading is grammatically possible and has ancient support: the Aramaic Targums (Onkelos and Neofiti) tend toward this reading, and it is the interpretation preferred by some rabbinic commentators. It is also exegetically significant: if the reward is God himself, the covenant theology of Genesis 15 is a theology of divine presence and relationship, not merely material blessing.

The crux is sharpened by the context. Genesis 14 concluded with Abram's categorical refusal of all reward from the king of Sodom. God now opens the next revelation with a declaration about מָגֵן (protection) and שָׂכָר (reward). If Abram refused the king of Sodom's offer of enrichment, God now offers himself as the true reward. The appositional reading fits this narrative logic elegantly.

The LXX renders: ἐγὼ ὑπερασπίζω σου· ὁ μισθός σου πολὺς ἔσται σφόδρα (ego hyperaspizō sou; ho misthos sou polys estai sphodra, "I am your shield; your reward shall be very great"). The LXX treats these as two separate predications — supporting the traditional predicate reading. ὑπερασπίζω (hyperaspizō, "to shield, to protect") is the verbal form; ο μισθός (ho misthos, "the reward, the wage") represents שָׂכָר. The LXX's grammatical construal aligns with the formal translation's "I am your shield; your reward shall be very great."

The formal translation renders the two clauses as distinct predications: "I am your shield; your reward shall be very great." The appositional reading is noted as a theologically rich alternative.

**3. אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה — "O Lord GOD" (vv. 2, 8) — The Address Formula**

Abram's responses in verses 2 and 8 both open with the double address אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה (Adonai YHWH). This is the only place in Genesis where this combination appears. The form יְהוִה (with different vowels from the standard יְהוָה) is a textual convention: when the divine names Adonai and YHWH appear in combination, the Masoretes vocalized the Tetragrammaton with the vowels of Elohim (אֱלֹהִים) rather than Adonai, to distinguish the two divine names and prevent the awkward double reading "Lord Lord." This produces the written form יְהוִה, which is conventionally rendered in English translation as "GOD" (in small capitals) when following "Lord." The formal translation renders "O Lord GOD" — the standard English convention for this double formula. It signals the solemnity and intimacy of Abram's address: he calls on God by the most exalted possible combination of his relational name and covenant name.

**4. עֲרִירִי — "Childless" (v. 2) — The Plight of Heirlessness**

The word עֲרִירִי (ariri, "childless, stripped of children, bereft") is a striking and somewhat rare term. It occurs also in Jeremiah 22:30 of a man written off as childless — one whose line will not continue. The root may be related to עָרָה (arah, "to strip bare") — one who is stripped of children. Abram's use of this term to describe himself is an act of raw vulnerability: the man God called out of Ur to make into a great nation (Genesis 12:2) now confesses that he is childless and his household heir is a servant. The pathos is deliberate.

The LXX renders ἄτεκνος (atekos, "childless, without children") — a transparent equivalent. The formal translation renders "childless."

**5. בֶּן מֶשֶׁק בֵּיתִי — "The Heir of My House" / "Eliezer of Damascus" (v. 2) — A Difficult Syntax**

The phrase אֶת אֱלִיעֶזֶר דַּמֶּשֶׂק (et Eliezer Dammeseq) in verse 2 involves a problematic construction that has challenged interpreters since antiquity. Several readings have been proposed:

**(a) "Eliezer of Damascus"** — taking דַּמֶּשֶׂק as a place of origin. Eliezer was from Damascus; he is Abram's Damascus-born steward. This is the traditional reading and the most natural reading of the Hebrew name with a geographic designation.

**(b) A compressed phrase** — some read דַּמֶּשֶׂק as a contracted form meaning "the Damascene one," parallel to the earlier phrase בֶּן מֶשֶׁק בֵּיתִי (ben mesheq beiti), which itself is obscure. The phrase בֶּן מֶשֶׁק (ben mesheq) may mean "son of acquisition," i.e., the heir of the household estate, or it may be a technical term for a household manager (cf. Akkadian māšqu, "watering, administration"). The two phrases (ben mesheq / Eliezer Dammeseq) may represent a wordplay or alliterative pairing intended to identify the household steward by both function and origin.

The formal translation renders the verse as: "the heir of my house is Eliezer of Damascus" — identifying both the function (heir/steward of the household) and the person (Eliezer of Damascus), without attempting to resolve the syntactic compression of ben mesheq. The phrase "I continue childless" captures the meaning of הוֹלֵךְ עֲרִירִי (holekh ariri, literally "going childless" — the participle holekh, "going," used idiomatically for a continuing state).

**6. וְהֶאֱמִן בַּיהוָה וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לּוֹ צְדָקָה — "He Believed the LORD, and He Counted It to Him as Righteousness" (v. 6) — The Most Theologically Significant Verse in Genesis**

Verse 6 is one of the most theologically charged sentences in all of Scripture. Three apostolic writers cite it directly: Paul in Romans 4:3 and Galatians 3:6, and James in James 2:23. It stands at the center of the Reformation debates over justification and is the foundational text for the doctrine of imputed righteousness in Protestant theology. Every word requires careful attention.

**הֶאֱמִן (he'emin)** — the Hiphil form of the root אָמַן (aman). The Hiphil of aman has the meaning "to regard as firm/sure, to trust, to believe, to have confidence in." It is not mere intellectual assent but a settled trust and reliance — committing one's weight to something or someone regarded as firm and trustworthy (the same root gives us אָמֵן, amen). The preposition בְּ (be-) follows the verb here: הֶאֱמִן בַּיהוָה — "he trusted in the LORD," "he believed the LORD." The ב preposition of trust means the believing is directed toward and rests upon YHWH himself, not merely a proposition about YHWH. The formal translation renders "he believed the LORD" — person-directed belief, not merely propositional assent.

The LXX renders: καὶ ἐπίστευσεν Αβραμ τῷ θεῷ (kai episteusen Abram to theo, "and Abram believed God") — using πιστεύω (pisteuō, "to believe, to have faith, to trust"), the verb from which the noun πίστις (pistis, "faith") derives. Paul's citations of this verse in Romans 4:3 and Galatians 3:6 quote the LXX directly: Ἐπίστευσεν δὲ Ἀβραὰμ τῷ θεῷ, καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην ("Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness"). The LXX is in full agreement with the MT in substance.

**וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לּוֹ (vayyachshveha lo)** — "and he counted it to him." The verb חָשַׁב (chashav) means "to reckon, to count, to calculate, to account." It is a forensic and commercial term — the ledger language of accounting. Something is placed to someone's account. The subject of "counted" is God (the LORD), and the object of counting is the feminine third-person pronominal suffix הָ (ha), which refers back to Abram's believing — his faith is what is being counted. The dative לּוֹ (lo, "to him") indicates the beneficiary: the counting is in Abram's favor, credited to him.

**צְדָקָה (tsedaqah)** — "righteousness." The word denotes conformity to a standard, right relationship, justice rightly ordered. In its forensic sense, it is the status of one who is in right standing — the condition of the one who is declared righteous in a legal or covenantal context. Crucially, Abram is not said to have righteousness as an innate quality; he is said to have had righteousness credited to him — רeckoned, imputed. The mechanism is faith: his trust in the LORD is what God reckon's as tsedaqah. This is the foundational pattern of what Paul in Romans 4 will call "the justification of the ungodly" (Romans 4:5) — the divine crediting of righteousness on the basis of faith rather than works.

The theological import of the verse is magnified by its location in the narrative. Abram has done nothing — no sacrifice, no circumcision, no law-keeping — in the immediate context of verse 6. He has simply believed the promise of verse 5 (that his offspring shall be as numerous as the stars). God counts this faith as righteousness. The subsequent covenant ceremony (vv. 9–17) and the later covenant of circumcision (Genesis 17) are both subsequent to and founded upon the prior declaration of verse 6. The formal translation renders each term with precision: "he believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness."

**7. הַנְּמָלָה חֲשֵׁכָה — "Dreadful and Great Darkness" / תַּרְדֵּמָה — "Deep Sleep" (v. 12)**

Verse 12 contains two distinct elements: a physical condition and a psychological-spiritual experience.

**תַּרְדֵּמָה (tardemah, "deep sleep")** — this is the same word used in Genesis 2:21, where God causes a deep sleep to fall upon Adam before taking his rib. It is not ordinary sleep (שֵׁנָה, sheinah) but a profound, God-induced unconsciousness or trance state that accompanies divine visitation. The LXX renders ἔκστασις (ekstasis, "ecstasy, trance, displacement of consciousness") — a term that in Greek describes a state of being outside oneself, which in this context captures the supernatural quality of Abram's experience.

**אֵימָה חֲשֵׁכָה גְדֹלָה (eimah chashekah gedolah, "a dreadful great darkness")** — the word אֵימָה (eimah) means "terror, dread, fear." It is not the ordinary word for darkness: it describes the quality of the darkness as awe-inspiring, overwhelming, dread-full. The three words together — "dreadful, dark, great" — pile up to describe an experience of overwhelming divine darkness. This is the numinous darkness that precedes theophany — the darkness before the fire (verse 17). The formal translation renders "dreadful and great darkness fell upon him" — preserving the word order and the triplet (dread / darkness / greatness) as best English idiom allows.

**8. אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה — "Four Hundred Years" (v. 13) and the Egyptian Prophecy**

The prophecy of the Egyptian bondage in verses 13–16 is one of the most precisely worded divine speeches in Genesis. God tells Abram three things with extraordinary specificity:

(1) His descendants will be sojourners (גֵּר, ger — a resident alien, a non-citizen) in a land not their own. The land is not named here; Egypt will be identified explicitly in the course of Exodus.

(2) They will serve the people of that land and be afflicted — עָנוּ אֹתָם (anu otam, "they will afflict them") — for four hundred years (אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה). The number four hundred appears here; Exodus 12:40 gives 430 years as the period of Israel's sojourn in Egypt. The difference is harmonized in various ways: some read the 400 years as rounding, others as dating from different starting points (Abraham's sojourn in Canaan as the beginning of sojourning, or from Isaac's birth, or from the actual entry into Egypt). The formal translation renders the text as given: "four hundred years."

(3) God will judge the nation they serve, and afterward they will come out with great possessions (רְכוּשׁ גָּדוֹל, rekhush gadol). The word רְכוּשׁ (rekhush, "possessions, property, wealth") links back to the possessions language of Genesis 14 (the same word was used for the spoils of the war of the kings). God promises a future exodus that mirrors Abram's own return from battle — with possessions.

(4) Abram himself will die in peace, buried at a good old age — בְּשֵׂיבָה טוֹבָה (besevah tovah, "at a good old age").

(5) The return will be in the fourth generation (הַדּוֹר הָרְבִיעִי, hador harevi'i) — "for the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete" (v. 16). The fullness of Amorite iniquity must be reached before the dispossession of the inhabitants of the land becomes the instrument of divine judgment. This statement is theologically important: the conquest of Canaan is not arbitrary ethnic cleansing but the execution of divine judgment upon iniquity that has reached its fullness. The formal translation renders "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete."

**9. תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן וְלַפִּיד אֵשׁ — "A Smoking Fire Pot and a Flaming Torch" (v. 17) — The Unilateral Covenant Theophany**

Verse 17 is the theological climax of Genesis 15 and one of the most important verses in the entire Pentateuch for covenant theology. The physical description is precise:

**תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן (tannur ashan)** — a smoking fire pot, a kiln or furnace (תַּנּוּר, tannur — a cylindrical clay oven or furnace) that is emitting smoke (עָשָׁן, ashan). This is not a hearth fire but a furnace — an object of intense heat that produces thick smoke.

**לַפִּיד אֵשׁ (lapid esh)** — a flaming torch, a torch of fire. The לַפִּיד (lapid) is a torch — a hand-held flame, suggesting movement and directed travel.

Together these two objects — the smoking furnace and the flaming torch — pass between the pieces of the divided animals. This is the decisive act of the covenant ceremony.

The covenant ritual described in Genesis 15 is the berit (בְּרִית, covenant) ceremony known from Jeremiah 34:18–19, where the parties "cut a covenant" (כָּרַת בְּרִית, karat berit — the idiom is literally "to cut a covenant") by passing between the pieces of a slaughtered animal. The significance of passing between the pieces is solemn self-imprecation: the parties swear that if they violate the covenant, let them become like the divided animals. It is a death oath.

The theological significance of verse 17 is that only God — represented by the smoking fire pot and flaming torch — passes between the pieces. Abram is in a deep sleep (verse 12) and plays no active role in the ceremony. The covenant is unilateral: God alone takes the oath. God alone binds himself. Abram makes no reciprocal commitment in this ceremony. The Abrahamic covenant at this point is a one-party covenant — a divine commitment that does not depend on Abram's performance for its validity. This is the theological foundation of the unconditional nature of the Abrahamic covenant, as developed by Paul in Galatians 3:15–18: the covenant was established 430 years before the law, and the law cannot annul it, because it was God's own sworn commitment, not a bilateral contract with conditions.

The fire and smoke also represent the mode of divine theophany throughout the Hebrew Bible: the pillar of fire and cloud in the Exodus (Exodus 13:21–22), the fire on Mount Sinai (Exodus 19:18: "Mount Sinai was wrapped in smoke because the LORD descended on it in fire"), the fire of the burning bush (Exodus 3:2). God consistently appears in fire and smoke when he makes his most solemn self-disclosures.

The LXX renders τέφρα καπνιζομένη (tephra kapnizoumene, "a smoking furnace") and λαμπὰς πυρός (lampas pyros, "a torch of fire") — in substantial agreement with the MT.

**10. כָּרַת בְּרִית — "Made a Covenant" (v. 18) — The Covenant Formula**

The phrase כָּרַת יְהוָה אֶת אַבְרָם בְּרִית (karat YHWH et Avram berit, "the LORD cut a covenant with Abram") uses the standard idiom for covenant-making in Hebrew: כָּרַת בְּרִית (karat berit, "to cut a covenant"). The verb כָּרַת (karat, "to cut") reflects the practice visible in verses 9–10: the covenant is ratified by the cutting of animals. To "cut a covenant" is to make one by the method of divided animals. The English idiom "made a covenant" is standard, but "cut a covenant" more precisely renders the Hebrew. The formal translation renders "made a covenant" — preserving the standard English idiom while the Notes record the precise Hebrew.

The LXX renders: διέθετο κύριος τῷ Αβραμ διαθήκην (dietheto kyrios to Abram diatheken, "the Lord established a covenant with Abram"). The LXX uses διατίθημι (diatithemi, "to arrange, to establish, to covenant") and διαθήκη (diatheke, "covenant, testament, disposition") — the Greek term that the New Testament will use throughout for the "new covenant" (new testament, διαθήκη καινή) established in Christ.

**11. מִנְּהַר מִצְרַיִם עַד הַנָּהָר הַגָּדֹל — "From the River of Egypt to the Great River, the River Euphrates" (v. 18) — The Land Boundaries**

The covenant grants land "from the river of Egypt" (נְהַר מִצְרַיִם, nahar Mitsrayim) to the Euphrates (נְהַר פְּרָת, nahar Parat). The "river of Egypt" (nahar Mitsrayim) is almost certainly the Wadi el-Arish — the seasonal watercourse in the Sinai that marked the traditional southwestern boundary of Canaan — rather than the Nile River proper. The "great river, the River Euphrates" (הַנָּהָר הַגָּדֹל נְהַר פְּרָת) is the Euphrates of Mesopotamia — the northeastern boundary. This "from Egypt to the Euphrates" formulation is the broadest description of the promised land in the Hebrew Bible. It represents a territory that at its maximal historical extent Israel only approached under David and Solomon (cf. 1 Kings 4:21: "Solomon ruled over all the kingdoms from the Euphrates to the land of the Philistines and to the border of Egypt").

**12. עֶשֶׂרֶת הַגּוֹיִם — "Ten Nations" (vv. 19–21) — The Peoples of the Land**

The closing list of peoples (vv. 19–21) names ten peoples whose territories constitute the covenanted land: Kenites, Kenizzites, Kadmonites, Hittites, Perizzites, Rephaim, Amorites, Canaanites, Girgashites, and Jebusites. This is the longest list of such peoples in Genesis. The standard "seven nations" formula familiar from Deuteronomy 7:1 is here expanded to ten, suggesting a more comprehensive geographic scope or a different organizing tradition. The number ten may carry symbolic weight (a complete enumeration), or it may reflect a genuine historical tradition of the ethnic complexity of the Canaanite region.

Of the ten, the Kenites and Kenizzites and Kadmonites are unique to this list — they do not appear in the standard Deuteronomic enumeration. The Rephaim (רְפָאִים) are the pre-Israelite giant clans attested in the Transjordan (Deuteronomy 3:11, 13; Genesis 14:5). The formal translation renders all proper names in their standard English forms.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 15

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 10–11 (GEN_015_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_015_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The chapter marker "Γ CAP. XV" is visible at the right margin of page 10, transitioning from Genesis 14. The following variants from the MT are noted:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 15:1 | אָנֹכִי מָגֵן לָךְ שְׂכָרְךָ הַרְבֵּה מְאֹד ("I am your shield; your reward shall be very great") | ἐγὼ ὑπερασπίζω σου· ὁ μισθός σου πολὺς ἔσται σφόδρα ("I shield you; your reward shall be very great") | "I am your shield; your reward shall be very great" | LXX uses verbal form ὑπερασπίζω ("to shield") rather than a nominal predicate; MT nominal form (מָגֵן, participle used as predicate noun) rendered as "I am your shield" |
| 15:1 | בַּמַּחֲזֶה ("in a vision") | ἐν ὁράματι ("in a vision") | "in a vision" | Full agreement |
| 15:6 | וְהֶאֱמִן בַּיהוָה ("and he believed the LORD") | καὶ ἐπίστευσεν τῷ θεῷ ("and he believed God") | "he believed the LORD" | LXX renders YHWH as ὁ θεός ("God") by standard convention; substance preserved; MT names YHWH explicitly |
| 15:6 | וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לּוֹ צְדָקָה ("and he counted it to him as righteousness") | καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην ("and it was counted to him as righteousness") | "he counted it to him as righteousness" | LXX uses passive form (ἐλογίσθη, "it was counted") versus MT's active form with implied divine subject; Paul's citation in Romans 4:3 and Galatians 3:6 follows LXX passive; MT active form (with God as the one who counts) is arguably more theologically precise — it is God who does the reckoning |
| 15:12 | תַּרְדֵּמָה ("deep sleep") | ἔκστασις ("ecstasy, trance") | "deep sleep" | LXX's ἔκστασις captures the visionary, trance-like quality; MT tardemah (same word as Gen 2:21) emphasizes the deep, God-induced unconsciousness; "deep sleep" preserved from MT |
| 15:13 | וַעֲבָדוּם וְעִנּוּ אֹתָם ("and they will serve them and they will afflict them") | καὶ δουλεύσουσιν αὐτοῖς καὶ ταπεινώσουσιν αὐτούς ("and they will serve them and they will humble them") | "serve... afflicted" | LXX uses ταπεινόω ("to humble, to humiliate") for עָנָה ("to afflict, to oppress"); MT's "afflict" is more physical; LXX's "humble" more relational — formal translation follows MT |
| 15:17 | תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן ("a smoking fire pot") | κλίβανος καπνιζόμενος ("a smoking furnace/oven") | "a smoking fire pot" | LXX κλίβανος (furnace, baking oven) is a reasonable equivalent for תַּנּוּר (tannur, a cylindrical clay oven/furnace); "fire pot" chosen to distinguish from bread-baking associations |
| 15:18 | כָּרַת יְהוָה אֶת אַבְרָם בְּרִית ("the LORD cut a covenant with Abram") | διέθετο κύριος τῷ Αβραμ διαθήκην ("the Lord established a covenant with Abram") | "made a covenant" | LXX's διαθήκη (diatheke) is the term used throughout the NT for "covenant/testament"; MT karat berit ("cut a covenant") preserved in Notes; translation follows standard English idiom |
| 15:19–21 | Ten peoples including Kenites, Kenizzites, Kadmonites | LXX includes Κιναίους, Κενεζαίους, Κεδμωναίους | Ten peoples as MT | Full agreement on list; LXX provides Greek transliterations |

The most theologically significant LXX variant is at verse 6: the LXX passive form ἐλογίσθη ("it was counted") versus the MT active form וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ ("and he counted it"). The MT active form makes God the explicit subject of the reckoning — it is YHWH who does the accounting, who counts Abram's faith as righteousness. The LXX passive, while producing the same theological result (righteousness is credited), subtly obscures the identity of the agent. The NT citations (Romans 4:3; Galatians 3:6; James 2:23) all follow the LXX passive. The formal translation renders the MT active: "he counted it to him as righteousness" — with the divine subject implied from the context.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 15 — The Covenant in Three Acts**

Genesis 15 is the theological center of gravity for the Abrahamic covenant cycle. While Genesis 12 gives the promise, and Genesis 17 institutes the covenant of circumcision, Genesis 15 provides the foundational covenant act that binds the entire narrative: the divine oath taken by God alone, under fire and smoke, while Abram sleeps. The chapter falls into three structural movements:

**Act 1 — Promise and Faith (vv. 1–6):** A divine vision opens with fear addressed and the shield/reward promise given. Abram's lament about childlessness twice breaks through (vv. 2–3), and God responds first with a word about an heir from Abram's own body (v. 4), then with an enacted sign — he leads Abram outside and shows him the stars. "So shall your offspring be." And Abram believed. And God counted that faith as righteousness. The movement from divine speech to faith to divine reckoning (vv. 1–6) is the theological core of the chapter: it establishes that the covenant relationship between God and Abram rests not on Abram's achievement but on his trust.

**Act 2 — Land Promise and Covenant Preparation (vv. 7–12):** God reminds Abram of his founding act: "I am the LORD who brought you out from Ur of the Chaldeans to give you this land." Abram asks for a sign of certainty: "How am I to know that I shall possess it?" God answers not with words but with a ceremony. Abram is instructed to bring specific animals — three species, three years old, plus two birds — and to cut them in two. This is the preparatory rite of the ancient covenant ceremony, the karat berit. Abram arranges the pieces but cannot complete the ceremony: at sunset, the deep sleep falls on him, and with it the dreadful darkness. He is rendered passive. The covenant will be completed not by his agency but by God's alone.

**Act 3 — Prophecy, Theophany, and Covenant Ratification (vv. 13–21):** In the deep sleep, God speaks: the Egyptian sojourn is prophesied with precise detail (400 years, affliction, judgment of Egypt, exodus with possessions, Abram's peaceful death, return in the fourth generation). Then, at full darkness, the smoking fire pot and the flaming torch pass between the pieces. God passes between the cut animals. He takes the death oath alone. The covenant is declared (v. 18): the land from Egypt to the Euphrates is given to Abram's offspring. The chapter closes with the ten peoples enumerated — the current possessors of the land that will eventually be theirs.

**The Theological Shape of the Abrahamic Covenant**

Genesis 15 establishes the Abrahamic covenant as distinctively unconditional. The structure of the ceremony — God alone passing between the pieces while Abram sleeps — is the textual demonstration of this unilaterality. Paul's argument in Galatians 3:15–18 depends precisely on this: the covenant was enacted as a promise (ἐπαγγελία, epangelia) and as a single-party commitment, not a bilateral contract. The law, coming 430 years later, cannot introduce conditions into a covenant whose ratification involved no conditions on Abram's part.

This covenant in Genesis 15 stands in tension-filled relationship with the conditional elements of the Mosaic covenant (Deuteronomy 28) and even with the sign-requirement of Genesis 17 (circumcision as the sign of covenant membership). The theological tradition has handled this tension by distinguishing between the unconditional grant of the promises (Genesis 15) and the conditional application of covenant membership to particular individuals (Genesis 17; Mosaic covenant). The formal translation renders Genesis 15 without attempting to resolve the subsequent theological discussion, preserving the text's own emphasis on divine initiative and unilateral commitment.

**The Star Promise and Righteousness by Faith**

The connective tissue between verse 5 (the star promise) and verse 6 (the faith/righteousness reckoning) is often passed over in commentary but is structurally vital. What Abram believed in verse 6 is not a general truth about God but the specific promise just given in verse 5: that his offspring will be as the stars — innumerable. This is an outrageous claim in the ears of a childless man past normal age. To believe it is to trust not the evidence of circumstances but the word of God. Paul in Romans 4:18–22 makes exactly this point: "In hope he believed against hope, that he should become the father of many nations... He did not weaken in faith when he considered his own body, which was as good as dead, or when he considered the barrenness of Sarah's womb. No unbelief made him waver concerning the promise of God... fully convinced that God was able to do what he had promised." The faith that is counted as righteousness is not merely a general religious posture; it is trust in the specific, seemingly impossible promise of God.

**Verse 6 and the History of Interpretation**

The interpretation of Genesis 15:6 has been the most contested verse in the history of biblical theology, dividing Judaism and Christianity, Catholic and Protestant traditions, and even various Protestant traditions from one another. The key points of contention have been:

(1) **The subject of חָשַׁב ("counted/reckoned"):** Is it God who counts Abram's faith as righteousness, or is it Abram who counts something as righteousness? The Hebrew unambiguously makes God the subject: וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לּוֹ (vayyachshveha lo, "and he counted it to him") — the subject of the verb is the LORD from the previous clause, and the indirect object לּוֹ (lo, "to him") is Abram. God does the reckoning. God credits the righteousness. This is the formal translation's rendering.

(2) **The object of חָשַׁב:** What is "it" that is counted as righteousness? The feminine pronominal suffix הָ (ha) on the verb וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ refers back to the believing (הֱאֱמִין, he'emin). His faith — the act and disposition of trusting God — is what is counted as righteousness. Not his circumcision (Paul's point in Romans 4:9–12: this happened before circumcision). Not his works. Not any merit of achievement. His faith.

(3) **The meaning of "as righteousness":** Does this mean God treated Abram's faith as if it were righteousness (imputation), or that faith actually is righteousness (identification), or that God rewarded faith with righteousness (transference)? The formal translation renders "as righteousness" — the standard rendering of לְ (le-) with a noun in this type of construction (the "lamed of specification" or "lamed of transformation"), which has the sense of "counted in the category of / reckoned to the account of." The formal translation does not adjudicate the imputation debate; it renders the text.

The formal translation renders: **"And he believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness."** Every word is chosen with care for the original, and the Notes record the full range of the theological issues the text raises.
